verses.text: Dying, David charges Solomon, 'Be thou strong, shew thyself a man,' and above all keep God's law, that he may prosper and the promise to David's line stand. A father's last words point his son not to power but to obedience. The path to true success is faithfulness to God's word.

Dying, David charges Solomon, 'Be thou strong, shew thyself a man,' and above all keep God's law, that he may prosper and the promise to David's line stand. A father's last words point his son not to power but to obedience. The path to true success is faithfulness to God's word.

David also leaves Solomon hard counsel about Joab and Shimei, men whose old crimes still await justice. Some wrongs deferred must finally be addressed by the new reign. A kingdom cannot be built securely while bloodguilt and treachery go forever unanswered.

David dies after forty years and is buried in his city, and 'Solomon sat upon the throne of David his father; and his kingdom was established greatly.' The great king passes; the LORD's purpose does not. God buries His workmen and carries His work on through the next.

Adonijah comes to Bathsheba claiming the kingdom was once his by right, though God had given it to Solomon. Even after defeat, his heart still circles the throne. A man who will not truly let go of his ambition is only waiting for the next chance to grasp.

Adonijah comes to Bathsheba claiming the kingdom was once his by right, though God had given it to Solomon. Even after defeat, his heart still circles the throne. A man who will not truly let go of his ambition is only waiting for the next chance to grasp.

He asks, through Bathsheba, for Abishag, David's last attendant, to be his wife. The request seems small, but to take a king's woman was to lay claim to the kingdom itself. Sin often makes its boldest moves under a modest, innocent-sounding cover.

Bathsheba brings the seemingly harmless petition to Solomon, who receives his mother with honour and sets her at his right hand. The king listens respectfully — then weighs what the request really means. Wisdom honours the messenger yet still tests the substance of the plea.

Bathsheba brings the seemingly harmless petition to Solomon, who receives his mother with honour and sets her at his right hand. The king listens respectfully — then weighs what the request really means. Wisdom honours the messenger yet still tests the substance of the plea.

Solomon sees through it at once: to ask for Abishag is to ask for the kingdom, and Adonijah's old allies stand behind him. He orders the execution. Mercy once offered on condition of good conduct is justly withdrawn when the rebel proves his heart unchanged.

Abiathar the priest, who had backed Adonijah, is spared death for past loyalty but banished from the priesthood — fulfilling the old word of judgment on Eli's house. Past faithfulness tempers his sentence, yet sin still costs him his office. God's old prophecies come due in time.

Abiathar the priest, who had backed Adonijah, is spared death for past loyalty but banished from the priesthood — fulfilling the old word of judgment on Eli's house. Past faithfulness tempers his sentence, yet sin still costs him his office. God's old prophecies come due in time.

Joab, hearing the news, flees to the altar and clings to its horns, but Solomon orders Benaiah to execute him there. The altar cannot shelter a murderer who never repented. Religion grasped only as an escape from justice is no refuge; God's mercy is not a hiding place for the unrepentant.

Joab, hearing the news, flees to the altar and clings to its horns, but Solomon orders Benaiah to execute him there. The altar cannot shelter a murderer who never repented. Religion grasped only as an escape from justice is no refuge; God's mercy is not a hiding place for the unrepentant.

Solomon explains that Joab's own blood-guilt — the treacherous murders of Abner and Amasa — returns on his head, clearing David's house of it. Long-delayed justice arrives at last. The blood of the innocent cries out until it is answered; God's accounts are settled in His time.

Solomon confines Shimei to Jerusalem on pain of death, and Shimei agrees — but three years later chases runaway servants to Gath, breaking his word. Given a clear boundary and a clear warning, he steps over it. Mercy with conditions tests whether the heart has truly changed.

Solomon summons Shimei: did I not bind you by an oath before the LORD not to leave the city? Shimei has no defence; he heard the terms and broke them. We cannot plead ignorance of a warning we plainly received and freely accepted.

Solomon summons Shimei: did I not bind you by an oath before the LORD not to leave the city? Shimei has no defence; he heard the terms and broke them. We cannot plead ignorance of a warning we plainly received and freely accepted.

Solomon summons Shimei: did I not bind you by an oath before the LORD not to leave the city? Shimei has no defence; he heard the terms and broke them. We cannot plead ignorance of a warning we plainly received and freely accepted.

Shimei is executed for his broken oath and old cursing of David, 'and the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon.' With justice done, the reign is secure. A throne — and a life — is settled only when what is owed to righteousness has been fully paid.
